channel.title: TinkerBench
channel.description: Honest reviews of soldering stations, bench multimeters, entry-level oscilloscopes, starter kits, and the small-batch tools that actually matter. Written by someone who spent 40 years in electronics a

item.title: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi: Choosing the Right Platform for Your Project
item.description: When to use Arduino, when to use Raspberry Pi, and when to use neither — a practical decision framework for maker projects.

item.title: Getting Started with Soldering: Complete Beginner Workbench Setup
item.description: Everything you need for a first soldering workbench: iron, tip, flux, solder type, and fume extraction — prioritized by what matters most.

item.title: Bench Power Supply Guide: From $30 to $300, What You Actually Need
item.description: Variable bench power supplies for hobbyists: voltage range, current limiting, display accuracy, and which features are actually useful.

item.title: 1. The best soldering iron for beginners: 30 vs 60 vs 200 dollar tiers
10. Fluke 117 vs Amprobe AM-570: when the 5x price is worth it

27. Filament storage: dry boxes vs vacuum sealing

29. The best Arduino starter kit for a 12 year old
3. Why your cheap soldering iron ruins every joint and how to fix it
30. Raspberry Pi 5 vs Pi 4 for hobbyist projects
31. ESP32 starter kits: what to buy and what to skip
32. Kit brands ranked: Elegoo vs SunFounder vs Osoyoo
33. Breadboard quality matters more than you think
34. 555 timer projects that still teach well in 2026
35. FPGA dev boards for beginners: iCE40 vs ECP5 vs Artix
36. Ham radio for the electronics hobbyist: a 10-hour starter path

38. PCB vises and helping hands that do not fall apart
39. Fume extractors: DIY vs store-bought vs nothing

40. Best magnifying lamps for benchwork
41. ESD mats and straps: who needs them and who is fine without

43. Five tools a beginner does not need to buy yet

Hakko FX-888D Digital Soldering Station with FX-8801 Tip
item.description: Is the Hakko FX-888D worth $110 over a $25 iron? We test tip temperature accuracy, recovery time, and 5-year durability data.

item.title: Helping Hands Soldering Third Hand Tool with Magnifying Glass
item.description: Third hand tools for soldering: the difference between a flimsy $8 version and a $22 one with a stable base.

item.title: FNIRSI DSO-TC3 Handheld Digital Oscilloscope and Component Tester
item.description: A $70 handheld oscilloscope and component tester: bandwidth limits, use cases where it works, and where it falls short.

item.title: ELEGOO UNO R3 Project Complete Starter Kit with Tutorial
item.description: The ELEGOO UNO R3 kit vs. buying components individually: what you actually get, what to buy next, and how far it takes a beginner.

item.title: Pine64 Pinecil Smart Mini Portable Soldering Iron V2
item.description: Pinecil V2 vs TS101 vs Hakko FX-888D: which portable iron wins for field repairs and travel maker work.

item.title: Best 3D Printer Under $300: Our Top Picks for Hobbyists
item.description: After 3 months of testing 12 budget 3D printers, we recommend the Creality Ender 3 V2 for beginners and the Anycubic Kobra Go for faster printing. See filament costs, failure rates, and upgrade paths.

item.title: Is an Arduino Starter Kit Worth Buying in 2024?
item.description: We tested seven Arduino starter kits over 90 days to determine which provide real value for makers versus overpriced bundles. Here's the breakdown of components, hidden costs, and alternatives.

item.title: How to Solder Through-Hole Components Like a Pro
item.description: Learn the right way to solder through-hole components with our tested tool recommendations and techniques. We compare irons, fluxes, and accessories to help you avoid cold joints and damaged PCBs.

item.title: Unlocking Your Circuits: A Guide to USB Logic Analyzers (24MHz)
item.description: Choosing a USB logic analyzer involves more than just sampling rate. We tested 8 models for signal integrity, software compatibility, and durability to help you find the right 24MHz analyzer for debugging Arduino, Raspberry Pi, and FPGA projects.

item.title: Is That Arduino Starter Kit Worth Buying? Our Top Picks
item.description: Choosing your first Arduino kit is overwhelming. We tested 12 popular kits for 30+ days each, evaluating component quality, documentation, and real project viability. Here’s what actually works for hobbyists.

item.title: Are Arduino Starter Kits Worth Buying? Honest Reviews for Beginners and Returning Makers
item.description: Arduino starter kits promise an easy entry into electronics, but are they worth the price? We bench-tested three popular kits with beginners and experienced makers, measured component durability, and mapped exactly when each kit pays for itself.

item.title: The Best Soldering Irons for Beginners: A Comprehensive Guide
item.description: Choosing your first soldering iron? We tested 15 models for heat recovery, tip longevity, and ergonomics to find the best options under $100 for hobbyists and makers.

item.title: Best Soldering Kits for Beginners: 30-Day Bench Test Results
item.description: Tested 18 beginner soldering kits on actual projects. The Pinecil V2 and X-Tronic 3020-XTS outperformed $300 stations. Learn which tools last, which fail fast, and why cheap irons destroy more boards than they build.

item.title: Bench Multimeter vs. Handheld: Which One Do You Need?
item.description: Choosing between a bench multimeter and a handheld model? This guide breaks down the key differences in accuracy, durability, and cost to help you make an informed decision for your electronics projects.

item.title: Bench Multimeter vs. Handheld: Which One Should You Buy?
item.description: Choosing between a bench multimeter and a handheld model depends on your needs. This guide compares accuracy, durability, and cost to help you make an informed decision.

item.title: Bench Multimeter vs. Handheld: Which One Should Your Workbench Have?
item.description: Choosing between a bench multimeter and a handheld depends on your workspace, budget, and measurement precision needs. We break down the real differences, costs, and best use cases for each type.

item.title: Top 3D Printers Under $300: Affordable Entry into Fabrication
item.description: After 90 days testing budget 3D printers, we found the Creality Ender 3 V3 SE balances price and performance best — but the Anycubic Kobra 2 Neo wins for speed. See print samples and cost breakdowns.

item.title: Best 3D Printer Under $300: Seven Budget Models Tested for Real-World Use
item.description: After 120 hours of test prints across seven sub-$300 3D printers, we recommend the Creality Ender 3 V3 SE for most hobbyists and retired engineers building custom enclosures and prototypes. We also tested the Anycubic Kobra Go, Elegoo Neptune 4, and Sovol SV01 to show you which budget printer works best for your projects—and which ones to avoid.

item.title: Best Benchtop Power Supply for Hobbyists: Clean Power for Your Projects
item.description: A proper benchtop power supply is the foundation of any electronics workbench. We've tested the top models—from the budget Mastech to the lab-grade Rigol—and show you which one matches your projects and budget.

item.title: The Best Soldering Irons for Beginners: A Comprehensive Guide
item.description: Choosing the right soldering iron as a beginner can be overwhelming. We compare the top models for ease of use, durability, and cost to help you make an informed decision.

item.title: Best Soldering Irons for Beginners: 30-Hour Bench Tests of the Hakko, Pinecil, and Budget Models
item.description: Choosing your first soldering iron? We tested four models for 30+ hours each on Arduino boards, guitar pedals, and vintage radios. Here's what maintains temperature, what fails first, and what's worth the extra cost.

item.title: The Best Soldering Irons for Beginners: Your First Step into Electronics
item.description: Choosing your first soldering iron? We tested 8 models under $100 for temperature stability, tip longevity, and ease of use. Here’s how the Hakko FX-888D, Pinecil, and budget options stack up for hobbyists.

item.title: Finding the Cheapest Decent Oscilloscope: A Buyer's Guide
item.description: Cut through the marketing hype with our hands-on testing of affordable oscilloscopes. We compare bandwidth, sample rates, and durability to find tools that won't fail your bench projects.

item.title: Finding the Cheapest Decent Oscilloscope: Reviews and Recommendations
item.description: After burning through three cheap oscilloscopes in 18 months, I tested eight models under $500 to find which actually work for hobby electronics. Here's what survives bench testing without breaking the bank.

item.title: Best Budget Oscilloscope Under $500: Real Bandwidth, Real Results
item.description: Most budget oscilloscopes lie about bandwidth. We tested six models under $500 with calibrated RF equipment to separate real measurement tools from spec-inflated toys—with hard data on which three actually work for Arduino, power supplies, and protocol decoding.

item.title: The Cheapest Decent Oscilloscope for Your Bench: Tested Under $300
item.description: Budget oscilloscopes range from $89 USB dongles to $400 four-channel units. We burned through 200+ hours testing six popular models on real hobbyist projects—Arduino PWM, switch-mode supplies, I2C debugging—to find where the sweet spot between price and usability actually sits.

item.title: Choosing Your First Oscilloscope: A Beginner's Guide
item.description: Picking your first oscilloscope doesn't have to be overwhelming. This guide compares key models, explains real-world performance tradeoffs, and helps you avoid common beginner mistakes.

item.title: Hakko FX-888D vs. Pinecil: Which Soldering Iron is Right for You?
item.description: Choosing between the Hakko FX-888D and Pinecil soldering irons? We compare performance, cost, and real-world usability to help you decide which tool fits your electronics projects best.

item.title: Hakko FX-888D vs. Pinecil: Which Soldering Iron Belongs on Your Bench?
item.description: The Hakko FX-888D is a proven workhorse; the Pinecil offers USB-C portability at a fraction of the cost. We tested both irons through 30+ days of real projects to help you pick the right tool for your bench.

item.title: Choosing the Best Benchtop Power Supply for Your Hobby Bench
item.description: Selecting the right benchtop power supply can make or break your electronics projects. We compare top models, analyze performance, and break down costs to help you find the perfect fit for your workbench.

item.title: Best Benchtop Power Supply for Hobbyists: Lab-Grade Precision Without the Lab-Grade Price
item.description: When wall adapters stop cutting it, you need a real benchtop power supply. We tested eight models across 30+ projects—from IoT prototypes to vintage synth repairs—to show you which deliver lab-grade precision and which waste money on noise and drift.

item.title: Mastering Through-Hole Soldering: A Step-by-Step Tutorial
item.description: This comprehensive guide walks through selecting the right soldering iron, proper technique for through-hole components, common mistakes to avoid, and maintenance tips to keep your tools in top shape.

item.title: How to Solder Through-Hole Components: Master the Essential Technique
item.description: Through-hole soldering is the foundation of reliable electronics work. This practical guide covers tool selection, proven techniques, and the mistakes that wreck pads—so you can build circuits that last decades instead of failing during debugging.

item.title: Pinecil vs. Hakko FX-888D: Which Soldering Iron is Right for You?
item.description: Choosing between the portable Pinecil and benchtop Hakko FX-888D? We tested both for 60+ hours of soldering to compare temperature control, tip options, durability, and total cost of ownership for different use cases.

item.title: Pinecil vs. Hakko FX-888D: Which Soldering Iron is Right for You?
item.description: Choosing between the Pinecil and Hakko FX-888D soldering irons? We compare their performance, durability, and cost to help you decide which tool fits your needs.

item.title: Kickstart Your Projects: Raspberry Pi 5 Ideas for Beginners
item.description: The Raspberry Pi 5 opens up a world of possibilities for hobbyists. We compare starter kits, break down real project costs, and share hard-won lessons from building 12 beginner projects over 90 days.

item.title: Best Raspberry Pi 5 Projects for Beginners: From Retro Gaming to Home Automation
item.description: The Raspberry Pi 5 is the most capable—and beginner-friendly—single-board computer yet. We've tested five complete projects: retro gaming console, Pi-hole ad blocker, home automation hub, weather station, and media server. Each includes component lists, performance benchmarks, and three budget tiers starting at $89.

item.title: How to Solder Through-Hole Components: A Step-by-Step Tutorial
item.description: Master through-hole soldering with our tested tool recommendations and step-by-step techniques. We compare Hakko, Weller, and budget options to help you build reliable circuits without cold joints or damaged components.

item.title: The Best 3D Printers Under $300: Affordable Entry into Additive Manufacturing
item.description: After 90 days of testing, the Creality Ender 3 V2 emerged as the best sub-$300 3D printer for beginners, balancing print quality and upgradability. But three other models deserve consideration depending on your workspace and tinkering tolerance.

item.title: Unlocking Digital Signals: Review of a 24MHz USB Logic Analyzer
item.description: We tested six 24MHz USB logic analyzers for signal accuracy, software compatibility, and durability across 30+ projects. Here’s how they stack up for Arduino, Raspberry Pi, and FPGA debugging.

item.title: Best 24MHz USB Logic Analyzers for Hobbyist Electronics Debugging
item.description: A 24MHz USB logic analyzer is the sweet spot for hobbyists debugging I2C, SPI, and UART signals without breaking the bank. We tested budget and mid-range models to see which captures fast signals reliably and which software actually works out of the box.

item.title: USB Logic Analyzers: Affordable Tools for Digital Debugging
item.description: From 8-channel workhorses to 24MHz sampling beasts, we bench-test seven USB logic analyzers under $200. Learn which models handle I2C debugging best, which software won't crash mid-capture, and when to step up to a professional tool.
